relevant_files.role: Core regex-based pattern engine that loads 14 vulnerability patterns from embedded JSON and scans code line-by-line
SecurityScanner orchestrator that coordinates pattern matching across PR diffs and file content with line number tracking
Type definitions for Finding, PatternDefinition, Severity, Confidence used throughout security module
Embedded JSON database of 14 security vulnerability patterns with regex, severity, CWE mappings
Detection logic that determines when security scans should be triggered based on PR metadata
LLM-based validator that reduces false positives by batching findings for AI validation
Performance benchmarks validating <10ms scan time for 500-line code samples
Integration tests covering hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, command injection detection
CLI integration using SecurityScanner in parallel across PR file patches

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with GPG signing and DCO sign-off required; format: type(scope): description
conventions.testing: AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert); unit tests in same file with #[cfg(test)], integration tests in tests/ directory; one happy path and one edge case per behavior
conventions.linting: cargo fmt --check for formatting, cargo clippy -- -D warnings for linting, cargo test for testing, cargo deny check for dependencies
conventions.error_handling: anyhow::Result for fallible operations; thiserror for library errors; Context trait for error wrapping; LazyLock for static initialization with error handling

patterns: Embedded resource loading via include_str! macro for patterns.json
LazyLock static initialization for global pattern engine singleton
Line-by-line regex scanning with file extension filtering
Parallel processing via rayon par_iter() for multi-file scanning
LLM-based false positive reduction via batched validation
Unified diff parsing with line number tracking for PR patches
SARIF report generation for standardized security output

related_issues.title: Add negative test case for multi-line vulnerability limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents known limitation that regex patterns cannot detect vulnerabilities spanning multiple lines; motivates tree-sitter evaluation

related_issues.title: PR with test for multi-line SQL injection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Test implementation demonstrating the gap tree-sitter would address

constraints: Performance target: <10ms for 500-line code samples (from benchmark)
Must support 14 existing vulnerability patterns with equivalent or better detection
Regex patterns currently operate line-by-line; multi-line detection is the primary gap
Binary size impact from grammar crates must be acceptable for CLI distribution
Hybrid approach preferred over full migration to minimize disruption
test_coverage: Comprehensive test coverage in crates/aptu-core/tests/security_integration.rs covering: hardcoded-api-key, hardcoded-password, sql-injection-concat, sql-injection-format, command-injection patterns. Unit tests in patterns.rs validate pattern loading, regex compilation, file extension filtering. Benchmark in benches/security_scan.rs validates performance on 500-line samples. Detection module has tests for label/keyword/path-based triggering logic.

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser::new(), Tree::root_node(), Node::child_count(), Node::kind(), Query API for pattern matching
library_findings.notes: Latest stable Rust bindings available on crates.io; supports incremental parsing and query-based AST traversal; requires language-specific grammar crates

library_findings.relevant_api: Provides Rust grammar for tree-sitter; enables AST parsing of Rust code
library_findings.notes: Available but version in issue references 0.23; current ecosystem supports multiple language grammars (python, javascript, etc.) as separate crates
Already used in patterns.rs; would be retained for simple patterns in hybrid approach

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner
approaches.description: Implement a new AstPatternEngine alongside existing PatternEngine. Create a pattern registry that marks patterns as regex-only or ast-enabled. For AST patterns, use tree-sitter queries (Scheme-like syntax) to match multi-line patterns; fall back to regex for simple patterns. Extend PatternDefinition to include optional tree-sitter query field. Create language-specific scanner variants (RustScanner, PythonScanner, etc.) that use appropriate grammar crates. Keep SecurityScanner as orchestrator, delegate to appropriate engine based on file type.
pros: Minimal disruption to existing regex infrastructure
Gradual migration path: convert patterns one at a time
Reuses existing validation and SARIF reporting
Can defer language support (start with Rust, add others later)
cons: Requires maintaining two pattern matching systems in parallel
Tree-sitter grammar crates add binary size (~5-15MB per language)
Team must learn tree-sitter query syntax (Scheme-like DSL)
Pattern definitions become more complex with dual regex/query fields
Increased test matrix (regex vs AST for each pattern)

approaches.name: Full Tree-Sitter Migration with Query Language
approaches.description: Replace PatternEngine entirely with AstPatternEngine using tree-sitter for all patterns. Rewrite all 14 patterns as tree-sitter queries in patterns.json (new 'query' field instead of 'pattern' field). Implement language-aware scanning that selects appropriate grammar based on file extension. Add a QueryEngine that compiles tree-sitter queries once at startup (similar to regex compilation). Extend SecurityScanner to auto-detect language and use corresponding parser. Implement data-flow analysis on top of AST for taint tracking (source-to-sink detection).
pros: Unified pattern matching system (simpler mental model)
True multi-line vulnerability detection with data-flow awareness
Language-aware parsing understands syntax, not just text patterns
Better performance for large files via incremental parsing
Scales to complex patterns (taint tracking, control flow)
cons: High complexity: requires rewriting all 14 patterns
Significant learning curve for tree-sitter query syntax
Binary size increase from multiple grammar crates (20-50MB+)
Performance regression risk during migration if queries not optimized
Requires comprehensive testing of AST-based patterns vs regex baselines
Data-flow analysis still needs custom logic on top of AST

approaches.name: Spike: Tree-Sitter for Rust SQL Injection Only
approaches.description: Minimal proof-of-concept: add tree-sitter-rust dependency, implement a single RustSqlInjectionDetector that uses tree-sitter queries to detect SQL injection via string concatenation and format! macros across multiple lines. Keep all other patterns in regex engine. Create a new scan_rust_sql_injection() method in SecurityScanner that runs AST-based detection for .rs files. Add comprehensive tests comparing regex vs AST detection on multi-line SQL injection fixtures. Benchmark performance vs regex baseline. Document findings and decision criteria for full migration.
pros: Minimal scope: single pattern, single language
Clear go/no-go decision point for full migration
Generates concrete performance and detection quality data
Team gains hands-on experience with tree-sitter API
cons: Doesn't solve the full problem (only SQL injection in Rust)
Introduces technical debt if not followed by full migration
Requires maintaining spike code or removing it later
Limited value unless followed by broader adoption

recommendation: Approach 1 (Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter) is recommended as the pragmatic path forward. It balances the need for multi-line vulnerability detection against implementation complexity and team learning curve. Start with a spike on SQL injection (Approach 3) to validate tree-sitter integration and performance, then use those findings to guide phased conversion of remaining patterns. This allows parallel regex/AST operation, reduces risk of regressions, and provides an escape hatch if tree-sitter proves problematic. The hybrid approach aligns with the issue's suggested 'Decide: Hybrid vs full migration' decision gate and respects the constraint that existing patterns must maintain <10ms performance. Prioritize converting the 2-3 patterns most likely to benefit from multi-line detection (SQL injection, command injection, path traversal) before evaluating full migration to Approach 2.
